old_column.content: At the very basic level, developing using Vite is not that different from using a static file server. However, Vite provides many enhancements over native ESM imports to support various features that are typically seen in bundler-based setups.

new_column.content: At the very basic level, developing using Vite is not that different from using a static file server. However, Vite provides many enhancements over native ESM imports to support various features that are typically seen in bundler-based setups.

old_column.content: Native ES imports do not support bare module imports like the following:

new_column.content: Native ES imports do not support bare module imports like the following:

old_column.content: The above will throw an error in the browser. Vite will detect such bare module imports in all served source files and perform the following:

new_column.content: The above import will throw an error in the browser. Vite will detect such bare module imports in all served source files and perform the following:

old_column.content: 1. [Pre-bundle](./dep-pre-bundling) them to improve page loading speed and convert CommonJS / UMD modules to ESM. The pre-bundling step is performed with [esbuild](https://esbuild.github.io/) and makes Vite's cold start time significantly faster than any JavaScript-based bundler.

new_column.content: 1. [Pre-bundle](./dep-pre-bundling) them to improve page loading speed and convert CommonJS / UMD modules to ESM. The pre-bundling step is performed with [esbuild](https://esbuild.github.io/) and makes Vite's cold start time significantly faster than any JavaScript-based bundler.

old_column.content: 2. Rewrite the imports to valid URLs like `/node_modules/.vite/deps/my-dep.js?v=f3sf2ebd` so that the browser can import them properly.

new_column.content: 2. Rewrite the imports to valid URLs like `/node_modules/.vite/deps/my-dep.js?v=f3sf2ebd` so that the browser can import them properly.

old_column.content: Vite caches dependency requests via HTTP headers, so if you wish to locally edit/debug a dependency, follow the steps [here](./dep-pre-bundling#browser-cache).

new_column.content: Vite caches dependency requests via HTTP headers, so if you wish to locally edit/debug a dependency, follow the steps [here](./dep-pre-bundling#browser-cache).

old_column.content: Vite provides an [HMR API](./api-hmr) over native ESM. Frameworks with HMR capabilities can leverage the API to provide instant, precise updates without reloading the page or blowing away application state. Vite provides first-party HMR integrations for [Vue Single File Components](https://github.com/vitejs/vite-plugin-vue/tree/main/packages/plugin-vue) and [React Fast Refresh](https://github.com/vitejs/vite-plugin-react/tree/main/packages/plugin-react). There are also official integrations for Preact via [@prefresh/vite](https://github.com/JoviDeCroock/prefresh/tree/main/packages/vite).

new_column.content: Vite provides an [HMR API](./api-hmr) over native ESM. Frameworks with HMR capabilities can leverage the API to provide instant, precise updates without reloading the page or blowing away application state. Vite provides first-party HMR integrations for [Vue Single File Components](https://github.com/vitejs/vite-plugin-vue/tree/main/packages/plugin-vue) and [React Fast Refresh](https://github.com/vitejs/vite-plugin-react/tree/main/packages/plugin-react). There are also official integrations for Preact via [@prefresh/vite](https://github.com/JoviDeCroock/prefresh/tree/main/packages/vite).

old_column.content: Note you don't need to manually set these up - when you [create an app via `create-vite`](./), the selected templates would have these pre-configured for you already.

new_column.content: Note you don't need to manually set these up - when you [create an app via `create-vite`](./), the selected templates would have these pre-configured for you already.

old_column.content: Vite supports importing `.ts` files out of the box.

new_column.content: Vite supports importing `.ts` files out of the box.

old_column.content: Note that Vite only performs transpilation on `.ts` files and does **NOT** perform type checking. It assumes type checking is taken care of by your IDE and build process.

new_column.content: Note that Vite only performs transpilation on `.ts` files and does **NOT** perform type checking. It assumes type checking is taken care of by your IDE and build process.

old_column.content: The reason Vite does not perform type checking as part of the transform process is because the two jobs work fundamentally differently. Transpilation can work on a per-file basis and aligns perfectly with Vite's on-demand compile model. In comparison, type checking requires knowledge of the entire module graph. Shoe-horning type checking into Vite's transform pipeline will inevitably compromise Vite's speed benefits.

new_column.content: The reason Vite does not perform type checking as part of the transform process is because the two jobs work fundamentally differently. Transpilation can work on a per-file basis and aligns perfectly with Vite's on-demand compile model. In comparison, type checking requires knowledge of the entire module graph. Shoe-horning type checking into Vite's transform pipeline will inevitably compromise Vite's speed benefits.

old_column.content: Vite's job is to get your source modules into a form that can run in the browser as fast as possible. To that end, we recommend separating static analysis checks from Vite's transform pipeline. This principle applies to other static analysis checks such as ESLint.

new_column.content: Vite's job is to get your source modules into a form that can run in the browser as fast as possible. To that end, we recommend separating static analysis checks from Vite's transform pipeline. This principle applies to other static analysis checks such as ESLint.

old_column.content: - For production builds, you can run `tsc --noEmit` in addition to Vite's build command.

new_column.content: - For production builds, you can run `tsc --noEmit` in addition to Vite's build command.

old_column.content: - During development, if you need more than IDE hints, we recommend running `tsc --noEmit --watch` in a separate process, or use [vite-plugin-checker](https://github.com/fi3ework/vite-plugin-checker) if you prefer having type errors directly reported in the browser.

new_column.content: - During development, if you need more than IDE hints, we recommend running `tsc --noEmit --watch` in a separate process, or use [vite-plugin-checker](https://github.com/fi3ework/vite-plugin-checker) if you prefer having type errors directly reported in the browser.

old_column.content: Vite uses [esbuild](https://github.com/evanw/esbuild) to transpile TypeScript into JavaScript which is about 20~30x faster than vanilla `tsc`, and HMR updates can reflect in the browser in under 50ms.

new_column.content: Vite uses [esbuild](https://github.com/evanw/esbuild) to transpile TypeScript into JavaScript which is about 20~30x faster than vanilla `tsc`, and HMR updates can reflect in the browser in under 50ms.

old_column.content: Use the [Type-Only Imports and Export](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/release-notes/typescript-3-8.html#type-only-imports-and-export) syntax to avoid potential problems like type-only imports being incorrectly bundled, for example:

new_column.content: Use the [Type-Only Imports and Export](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/release-notes/typescript-3-8.html#type-only-imports-and-export) syntax to avoid potential problems like type-only imports being incorrectly bundled, for example:

old_column.content: Vite respects some of the options in `tsconfig.json` and sets the corresponding esbuild options. For each file, Vite uses the `tsconfig.json` in the closest parent directory. If that `tsconfig.json` contains a [`references`](https://www.typescriptlang.org/tsconfig/#references) field, Vite will use the referenced config file that satisfies the [`include`](https://www.typescriptlang.org/tsconfig/#include) and [`exclude`](https://www.typescriptlang.org/tsconfig/#exclude) fields.

new_column.content: Vite respects some of the options in `tsconfig.json` and sets the corresponding esbuild options. For each file, Vite uses the `tsconfig.json` in the closest parent directory. If that `tsconfig.json` contains a [`references`](https://www.typescriptlang.org/tsconfig/#references) field, Vite will use the referenced config file that satisfies the [`include`](https://www.typescriptlang.org/tsconfig/#include) and [`exclude`](https://www.typescriptlang.org/tsconfig/#exclude) fields.

old_column.content: When the options are set in both the Vite config and the `tsconfig.json`, the value in the Vite config takes precedence.

new_column.content: When the options are set in both the Vite config and the `tsconfig.json`, the value in the Vite config takes precedence.

old_column.content: Some configuration fields under `compilerOptions` in `tsconfig.json` require special attention.

new_column.content: Some configuration fields under `compilerOptions` in `tsconfig.json` require special attention.

old_column.content: It is because `esbuild` only performs transpilation without type information, it doesn't support certain features like const enum and implicit type-only imports.

new_column.content: It is because `esbuild` only performs transpilation without type information, it doesn't support certain features like const enum and implicit type-only imports.

old_column.content: You must set `"isolatedModules": true` in your `tsconfig.json` under `compilerOptions`, so that TS will warn you against the features that do not work with isolated transpilation.

new_column.content: You must set `"isolatedModules": true` in your `tsconfig.json` under `compilerOptions`, so that TS will warn you against the features that do not work with isolated transpilation.

old_column.content: If a dependency doesn't work well with `"isolatedModules": true`. You can use `"skipLibCheck": true` to temporarily suppress the errors until it is fixed upstream.

new_column.content: If a dependency doesn't work well with `"isolatedModules": true`. You can use `"skipLibCheck": true` to temporarily suppress the errors until it is fixed upstream.

old_column.content: The default value will be `true` if the TypeScript target is `ES2022` or newer including `ESNext`. It is consistent with the [behavior of TypeScript 4.3.2+](https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript/pull/42663).

new_column.content: The default value will be `true` if the TypeScript target is `ES2022` or newer including `ESNext`. It is consistent with the [behavior of TypeScript 4.3.2+](https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript/pull/42663).

old_column.content: Other TypeScript targets will default to `false`.

new_column.content: Other TypeScript targets will default to `false`.

old_column.content: `true` is the standard ECMAScript runtime behavior.

new_column.content: `true` is the standard ECMAScript runtime behavior.

old_column.content: If you are using a library that heavily relies on class fields, please be careful about the library's intended usage of it.

new_column.content: If you are using a library that heavily relies on class fields, please be careful about the library's intended usage of it.

old_column.content: While most libraries expect `"useDefineForClassFields": true`, you can explicitly set `useDefineForClassFields` to `false` if your library doesn't support it.

new_column.content: While most libraries expect `"useDefineForClassFields": true`, you can explicitly set `useDefineForClassFields` to `false` if your library doesn't support it.

old_column.content: Vite ignores the `target` value in the `tsconfig.json`, following the same behavior as `esbuild`.

new_column.content: Vite ignores the `target` value in the `tsconfig.json`, following the same behavior as `esbuild`.

old_column.content: To specify the target in dev, the [`esbuild.target`](/config/shared-options.html#esbuild) option can be used, which defaults to `esnext` for minimal transpilation. In builds, the [`build.target`](/config/build-options.html#build-target) option takes higher priority over `esbuild.target` and can also be set if needed.

new_column.content: To specify the target in dev, the [`esbuild.target`](/config/shared-options.html#esbuild) option can be used, which defaults to `esnext` for minimal transpilation. In builds, the [`build.target`](/config/build-options.html#build-target) option takes higher priority over `esbuild.target` and can also be set if needed.

old_column.content: This option is only partially supported. Full support requires type inference by the TypeScript compiler, which is not supported. See [Oxc Transformer's documentation](https://oxc.rs/docs/guide/usage/transformer/typescript#decorators) for details.

new_column.content: This option is only partially supported. Full support requires type inference by the TypeScript compiler, which is not supported. See [Oxc Transformer's documentation](https://oxc.rs/docs/guide/usage/transformer/typescript#decorators) for details.

old_column.content: `resolve.tsconfigPaths: true` can be specified to tell Vite to use the `paths` option in `tsconfig.json` to resolve imports.

new_column.content: `resolve.tsconfigPaths: true` can be specified to tell Vite to use the `paths` option in `tsconfig.json` to resolve imports.

old_column.content: Note that this feature has a performance cost and is [discouraged by the TypeScript team to use this option to change the behavior of the external tools](https://www.typescriptlang.org/tsconfig/#paths:~:text=Note%20that%20this%20feature%20does%20not%20change%20how%20import%20paths%20are%20emitted%20by%20tsc%2C%20so%20paths%20should%20only%20be%20used%20to%20inform%20TypeScript%20that%20another%20tool%20has%20this%20mapping%20and%20will%20use%20it%20at%20runtime%20or%20when%20bundling.).

new_column.content: Note that this feature has a performance cost and is [discouraged by the TypeScript team to use this option to change the behavior of the external tools](https://www.typescriptlang.org/tsconfig/#paths:~:text=Note%20that%20this%20feature%20does%20not%20change%20how%20import%20paths%20are%20emitted%20by%20tsc%2C%20so%20paths%20should%20only%20be%20used%20to%20inform%20TypeScript%20that%20another%20tool%20has%20this%20mapping%20and%20will%20use%20it%20at%20runtime%20or%20when%20bundling.).

old_column.content: HTML files stand [front-and-center](/guide/#index-html-and-project-root) of a Vite project, serving as the entry points for your application, making it simple to build single-page and [multi-page applications](/guide/build.html#multi-page-app).

new_column.content: HTML files stand [front-and-center](/guide/#index-html-and-project-root) of a Vite project, serving as the entry points for your application, making it simple to build single-page and [multi-page applications](/guide/build.html#multi-page-app).

new_column.content: Any HTML files in your project root can be directly accessed by its respective directory path:

new_column.content: Assets referenced by HTML elements such as `<script type="module" src>` and `<link href>` are processed and bundled as part of the app. The full list of supported elements are as below:

new_column.content: To opt-out of HTML processing on certain elements, you can add the `vite-ignore` attribute on the element, which can be useful when referencing external assets or CDN.

old_column.content: All modern frameworks maintain integrations with Vite. Most framework plugins are maintained by each framework team, with the exception of the official Vue and React Vite plugins that are maintained in the vite org:

new_column.content: All modern frameworks maintain integrations with Vite. Most framework plugins are maintained by each framework team, with the exception of the official Vue and React Vite plugins that are maintained in the vite org:

old_column.content: Check out the [Plugins Guide](/plugins/) for more information.

new_column.content: Check out the [Plugins Guide](/plugins/) for more information.

old_column.content: `.jsx` and `.tsx` files are also supported out of the box. JSX transpilation is also handled via [esbuild](https://esbuild.github.io).

new_column.content: `.jsx` and `.tsx` files are also supported out of the box. JSX transpilation is also handled via [esbuild](https://esbuild.github.io).

old_column.content: Your framework of choice will already configure JSX out of the box (for example, Vue users should use the official [@vitejs/plugin-vue-jsx](https://github.com/vitejs/vite-plugin-vue/tree/main/packages/plugin-vue-jsx) plugin, which provides Vue 3 specific features including HMR, global component resolving, directives and slots).

new_column.content: Your framework of choice will already configure JSX out of the box (for example, Vue users should use the official [@vitejs/plugin-vue-jsx](https://github.com/vitejs/vite-plugin-vue/tree/main/packages/plugin-vue-jsx) plugin, which provides Vue 3 specific features including HMR, global component resolving, directives and slots).

old_column.content: If using JSX with your own framework, custom `jsxFactory` and `jsxFragment` can be configured using the [`esbuild` option](/config/shared-options.md#esbuild). For example, the Preact plugin would use:

new_column.content: If using JSX with your own framework, custom `jsxFactory` and `jsxFragment` can be configured using the [`esbuild` option](/config/shared-options.md#esbuild). For example, the Preact plugin would use:

old_column.content: You can inject the JSX helpers using `jsxInject` (which is a Vite-only option) to avoid manual imports:

new_column.content: You can inject the JSX helpers using `jsxInject` (which is a Vite-only option) to avoid manual imports:

old_column.content: Importing `.css` files will inject its content to the page via a `<style>` tag with HMR support.

new_column.content: Importing `.css` files will inject its content to the page via a `<style>` tag with HMR support.

old_column.content: Vite is pre-configured to support CSS `@import` inlining via `postcss-import`. Vite aliases are also respected for CSS `@import`. In addition, all CSS `url()` references, even if the imported files are in different directories, are always automatically rebased to ensure correctness.

new_column.content: Vite is pre-configured to support CSS `@import` inlining via `postcss-import`. Vite aliases are also respected for CSS `@import`. In addition, all CSS `url()` references, even if the imported files are in different directories, are always automatically rebased to ensure correctness.

old_column.content: `@import` aliases and URL rebasing are also supported for Sass and Less files (see [CSS Pre-processors](#css-pre-processors)).

new_column.content: `@import` aliases and URL rebasing are also supported for Sass and Less files (see [CSS Pre-processors](#css-pre-processors)).

old_column.content: If the project contains valid PostCSS config (any format supported by [postcss-load-config](https://github.com/postcss/postcss-load-config), e.g. `postcss.config.js`), it will be automatically applied to all imported CSS.

new_column.content: If the project contains valid PostCSS config (any format supported by [postcss-load-config](https://github.com/postcss/postcss-load-config), e.g. `postcss.config.js`), it will be automatically applied to all imported CSS.

old_column.content: Note that CSS minification will run after PostCSS and will use [`build.cssTarget`](/config/build-options.md#build-csstarget) option.

new_column.content: Note that CSS minification will run after PostCSS and will use [`build.cssTarget`](/config/build-options.md#build-csstarget) option.

old_column.content: Any CSS file ending with `.module.css` is considered a [CSS modules file](https://github.com/css-modules/css-modules). Importing such a file will return the corresponding module object:

new_column.content: Any CSS file ending with `.module.css` is considered a [CSS modules file](https://github.com/css-modules/css-modules). Importing such a file will return the corresponding module object:

old_column.content: CSS modules behavior can be configured via the [`css.modules` option](/config/shared-options.md#css-modules).

new_column.content: CSS modules behavior can be configured via the [`css.modules` option](/config/shared-options.md#css-modules).

old_column.content: If `css.modules.localsConvention` is set to enable camelCase locals (e.g. `localsConvention: 'camelCaseOnly'`), you can also use named imports:

new_column.content: If `css.modules.localsConvention` is set to enable camelCase locals (e.g. `localsConvention: 'camelCaseOnly'`), you can also use named imports:

old_column.content: Because Vite targets modern browsers only, it is recommended to use native CSS variables with PostCSS plugins that implement CSSWG drafts (e.g. [postcss-nesting](https://github.com/csstools/postcss-plugins/tree/main/plugins/postcss-nesting)) and author plain, future-standards-compliant CSS.

new_column.content: Because Vite targets modern browsers only, it is recommended to use native CSS variables with PostCSS plugins that implement CSSWG drafts (e.g. [postcss-nesting](https://github.com/csstools/postcss-plugins/tree/main/plugins/postcss-nesting)) and author plain, future-standards-compliant CSS.

old_column.content: That said, Vite does provide built-in support for `.scss`, `.sass`, `.less`, `.styl` and `.stylus` files. There is no need to install Vite-specific plugins for them, but the corresponding pre-processor itself must be installed:

new_column.content: That said, Vite does provide built-in support for `.scss`, `.sass`, `.less`, `.styl` and `.stylus` files. There is no need to install Vite-specific plugins for them, but the corresponding pre-processor itself must be installed:

old_column.content: If using Vue single file components, this also automatically enables `<style lang="sass">` et al.

new_column.content: If using Vue single file components, this also automatically enables `<style lang="sass">` et al.

old_column.content: Vite improves `@import` resolving for Sass and Less so that Vite aliases are also respected. In addition, relative `url()` references inside imported Sass/Less files that are in different directories from the root file are also automatically rebased to ensure correctness. Rebasing `url()` references that start with a variable or a interpolation are not supported due to its API constraints.

new_column.content: Vite improves `@import` resolving for Sass and Less so that Vite aliases are also respected. In addition, relative `url()` references inside imported Sass/Less files that are in different directories from the root file are also automatically rebased to ensure correctness. Rebasing `url()` references that start with a variable or a interpolation are not supported due to its API constraints.

old_column.content: `@import` alias and url rebasing are not supported for Stylus due to its API constraints.

new_column.content: `@import` alias and url rebasing are not supported for Stylus due to its API constraints.

old_column.content: You can also use CSS modules combined with pre-processors by prepending `.module` to the file extension, for example `style.module.scss`.

new_column.content: You can also use CSS modules combined with pre-processors by prepending `.module` to the file extension, for example `style.module.scss`.

old_column.content: ### Disabling CSS injection into the page

new_column.content: ### Disabling CSS injection into the page

old_column.content: The automatic injection of CSS contents can be turned off via the `?inline` query parameter. In this case, the processed CSS string is returned as the module's default export as usual, but the styles aren't injected to the page.

new_column.content: The automatic injection of CSS contents can be turned off via the `?inline` query parameter. In this case, the processed CSS string is returned as the module's default export as usual, but the styles aren't injected to the page.

old_column.content: import './foo.css' // will be injected into the page

new_column.content: import './foo.css' // will be injected into the page

old_column.content: import otherStyles from './bar.css?inline' // will not be injected

new_column.content: import otherStyles from './bar.css?inline' // will not be injected

old_column.content: Default and named imports from CSS files (e.g `import style from './foo.css'`) are removed since Vite 5. Use the `?inline` query instead.

new_column.content: Default and named imports from CSS files (e.g `import style from './foo.css'`) are removed since Vite 5. Use the `?inline` query instead.

old_column.content: Starting from Vite 4.4, there is experimental support for [Lightning CSS](https://lightningcss.dev/). You can opt into it by adding [`css.transformer: 'lightningcss'`](../config/shared-options.md#css-transformer) to your config file and install the optional [`lightningcss`](https://www.npmjs.com/package/lightningcss) dependency:

new_column.content: Starting from Vite 4.4, there is experimental support for [Lightning CSS](https://lightningcss.dev/). You can opt into it by adding [`css.transformer: 'lightningcss'`](../config/shared-options.md#css-transformer) to your config file and install the optional [`lightningcss`](https://www.npmjs.com/package/lightningcss) dependency:

old_column.content: If enabled, CSS files will be processed by Lightning CSS instead of PostCSS. To configure it, you can pass Lightning CSS options to the [`css.lightningcss`](../config/shared-options.md#css-lightningcss) config option.

new_column.content: If enabled, CSS files will be processed by Lightning CSS instead of PostCSS. To configure it, you can pass Lightning CSS options to the [`css.lightningcss`](../config/shared-options.md#css-lightningcss) config option.

old_column.content: To configure CSS Modules, you'll use [`css.lightningcss.cssModules`](https://lightningcss.dev/css-modules.html) instead of [`css.modules`](../config/shared-options.md#css-modules) (which configures the way PostCSS handles CSS modules).

new_column.content: To configure CSS Modules, you'll use [`css.lightningcss.cssModules`](https://lightningcss.dev/css-modules.html) instead of [`css.modules`](../config/shared-options.md#css-modules) (which configures the way PostCSS handles CSS modules).

old_column.content: By default, Vite uses esbuild to minify CSS. Lightning CSS can also be used as the CSS minifier with [`build.cssMinify: 'lightningcss'`](../config/build-options.md#build-cssminify).

new_column.content: By default, Vite uses esbuild to minify CSS. Lightning CSS can also be used as the CSS minifier with [`build.cssMinify: 'lightningcss'`](../config/build-options.md#build-cssminify).

new_column.content: You can also use the `base` option to provide base path for the imports:

old_column.content: The base option can only be a directory path relative to the importer file or absolute against the project root. Aliases and virtual modules aren't supported.

new_column.content: The base option can only be a directory path relative to the importer file or absolute against the project root. Aliases and virtual modules aren't supported.

old_column.content: Only the globs that are relative paths are interpreted as relative to the resolved base.

new_column.content: Only the globs that are relative paths are interpreted as relative to the resolved base.

old_column.content: All the resulting module keys are modified to be relative to the base if provided.

new_column.content: All the resulting module keys are modified to be relative to the base if provided.

old_column.content: - This is a Vite-only feature and is not a web or ES standard.

new_column.content: - This is a Vite-only feature and is not a web or ES standard.

old_column.content: - The glob patterns are treated like import specifiers: they must be either relative (start with `./`) or absolute (start with `/`, resolved relative to project root) or an alias path (see [`resolve.alias` option](/config/shared-options.md#resolve-alias)).

new_column.content: - The glob patterns are treated like import specifiers: they must be either relative (start with `./`) or absolute (start with `/`, resolved relative to project root) or an alias path (see [`resolve.alias` option](/config/shared-options.md#resolve-alias)).

old_column.content: - The glob matching is done via [`tinyglobby`](https://github.com/SuperchupuDev/tinyglobby) - check out its documentation for [supported glob patterns](https://superchupu.dev/tinyglobby/comparison).

new_column.content: - The glob matching is done via [`tinyglobby`](https://github.com/SuperchupuDev/tinyglobby) - check out its documentation for [supported glob patterns](https://superchupu.dev/tinyglobby/comparison).

old_column.content: - You should also be aware that all the arguments in the `import.meta.glob` must be **passed as literals**. You can NOT use variables or expressions in them.

new_column.content: - You should also be aware that all the arguments in the `import.meta.glob` must be **passed as literals**. You can NOT use variables or expressions in them.

old_column.content: Similar to [glob import](#glob-import), Vite also supports dynamic import with variables.

new_column.content: Similar to [glob import](#glob-import), Vite also supports dynamic import with variables.

old_column.content: Note that variables only represent file names one level deep. If `file` is `'foo/bar'`, the import would fail. For more advanced usage, you can use the [glob import](#glob-import) feature.

new_column.content: Note that variables only represent file names one level deep. If `file` is `'foo/bar'`, the import would fail. For more advanced usage, you can use the [glob import](#glob-import) feature.

old_column.content: Also note that the dynamic import must match the following rules to be bundled:

new_column.content: Also note that the dynamic import must match the following rules to be bundled:

old_column.content: - Imports must start with `./` or `../`: ``import(`./dir/${foo}.js`)`` is valid, but ``import(`${foo}.js`)`` is not.

new_column.content: - Imports must start with `./` or `../`: ``import(`./dir/${foo}.js`)`` is valid, but ``import(`${foo}.js`)`` is not.

old_column.content: - Imports must end with a file extension: ``import(`./dir/${foo}.js`)`` is valid, but ``import(`./dir/${foo}`)`` is not.

new_column.content: - Imports must end with a file extension: ``import(`./dir/${foo}.js`)`` is valid, but ``import(`./dir/${foo}`)`` is not.

old_column.content: - Imports to the own directory must specify a file name pattern: ``import(`./prefix-${foo}.js`)`` is valid, but ``import(`./${foo}.js`)`` is not.

new_column.content: - Imports to the own directory must specify a file name pattern: ``import(`./prefix-${foo}.js`)`` is valid, but ``import(`./${foo}.js`)`` is not.

old_column.content: These rules are enforced to prevent accidentally importing files that are not intended to be bundled. For example, without these rules, `import(foo)` would bundle everything in the file system.

new_column.content: These rules are enforced to prevent accidentally importing files that are not intended to be bundled. For example, without these rules, `import(foo)` would bundle everything in the file system.

old_column.content: Pre-compiled `.wasm` files can be imported with `?init`.

new_column.content: Pre-compiled `.wasm` files can be imported with `?init`.

old_column.content: The default export will be an initialization function that returns a Promise of the [`WebAssembly.Instance`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/WebAssembly/JavaScript_interface/Instance):

new_column.content: The default export will be an initialization function that returns a Promise of the [`WebAssembly.Instance`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/WebAssembly/JavaScript_interface/Instance):

new_column.content: The init function can also take an importObject which is passed along to [`WebAssembly.instantiate`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/WebAssembly/JavaScript_interface/instantiate) as its second argument:

old_column.content: In the production build, `.wasm` files smaller than `assetInlineLimit` will be inlined as base64 strings. Otherwise, they will be treated as a [static asset](./assets) and fetched on-demand.

new_column.content: In the production build, `.wasm` files smaller than `assetInlineLimit` will be inlined as base64 strings. Otherwise, they will be treated as a [static asset](./assets) and fetched on-demand.

new_column.content: [ES Module Integration Proposal for WebAssembly](https://github.com/WebAssembly/esm-integration) is not currently supported.

new_column.content: Use [`vite-plugin-wasm`](https://github.com/Menci/vite-plugin-wasm) or other community plugins to handle this.

old_column.content: A web worker script can be imported using [`new Worker()`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Worker/Worker) and [`new SharedWorker()`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/SharedWorker/SharedWorker). Compared to the worker suffixes, this syntax leans closer to the standards and is the **recommended** way to create workers.

new_column.content: A web worker script can be imported using [`new Worker()`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Worker/Worker) and [`new SharedWorker()`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/SharedWorker/SharedWorker). Compared to the worker suffixes, this syntax leans closer to the standards and is the **recommended** way to create workers.

old_column.content: const worker = new Worker(new URL('./worker.js', import.meta.url))

new_column.content: const worker = new Worker(new URL('./worker.js', import.meta.url))

old_column.content: The worker constructor also accepts options, which can be used to create "module" workers:

new_column.content: The worker constructor also accepts options, which can be used to create "module" workers:

old_column.content: const worker = new Worker(new URL('./worker.js', import.meta.url), {

new_column.content: const worker = new Worker(new URL('./worker.js', import.meta.url), {

old_column.content: The worker detection will only work if the `new URL()` constructor is used directly inside the `new Worker()` declaration. Additionally, all options parameters must be static values (i.e. string literals).

new_column.content: The worker detection will only work if the `new URL()` constructor is used directly inside the `new Worker()` declaration. Additionally, all options parameters must be static values (i.e. string literals).

old_column.content: A web worker script can be directly imported by appending `?worker` or `?sharedworker` to the import request. The default export will be a custom worker constructor:

new_column.content: A web worker script can be directly imported by appending `?worker` or `?sharedworker` to the import request. The default export will be a custom worker constructor:

old_column.content: The worker script can also use ESM `import` statements instead of `importScripts()`. **Note**: During development this relies on [browser native support](https://caniuse.com/?search=module%20worker), but for the production build it is compiled away.

new_column.content: The worker script can also use ESM `import` statements instead of `importScripts()`. **Note**: During development this relies on [browser native support](https://caniuse.com/?search=module%20worker), but for the production build it is compiled away.

old_column.content: By default, the worker script will be emitted as a separate chunk in the production build. If you wish to inline the worker as base64 strings, add the `inline` query:

new_column.content: By default, the worker script will be emitted as a separate chunk in the production build. If you wish to inline the worker as base64 strings, add the `inline` query:

new_column.content: If you wish to retrieve the worker as a URL, add the `url` query:

new_column.content: See [Worker Options](/config/worker-options.md) for details on configuring the bundling of all workers.

old_column.content: To deploy CSP, certain directives or configs must be set due to Vite's internals.

new_column.content: To deploy CSP, certain directives or configs must be set due to Vite's internals.

old_column.content: When [`html.cspNonce`](/config/shared-options#html-cspnonce) is set, Vite adds a nonce attribute with the specified value to any `<script>` and `<style>` tags, as well as `<link>` tags for stylesheets and module preloading. Additionally, when this option is set, Vite will inject a meta tag (`<meta property="csp-nonce" nonce="PLACEHOLDER" />`).

new_column.content: When [`html.cspNonce`](/config/shared-options#html-cspnonce) is set, Vite adds a nonce attribute with the specified value to any `<script>` and `<style>` tags, as well as `<link>` tags for stylesheets and module preloading. Additionally, when this option is set, Vite will inject a meta tag (`<meta property="csp-nonce" nonce="PLACEHOLDER" />`).

old_column.content: The nonce value of a meta tag with `property="csp-nonce"` will be used by Vite whenever necessary during both dev and after build.

new_column.content: The nonce value of a meta tag with `property="csp-nonce"` will be used by Vite whenever necessary during both dev and after build.

old_column.content: Ensure that you replace the placeholder with a unique value for each request. This is important to prevent bypassing a resource's policy, which can otherwise be easily done.

new_column.content: Ensure that you replace the placeholder with a unique value for each request. This is important to prevent bypassing a resource's policy, which can otherwise be easily done.

old_column.content: By default, during build, Vite inlines small assets as data URIs. Allowing `data:` for related directives (e.g. [`img-src`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Headers/Content-Security-Policy/img-src), [`font-src`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Headers/Content-Security-Policy/font-src)), or, disabling it by setting [`build.assetsInlineLimit: 0`](/config/build-options#build-assetsinlinelimit) is necessary.

new_column.content: By default, during build, Vite inlines small assets as data URIs. Allowing `data:` for related directives (e.g. [`img-src`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Headers/Content-Security-Policy/img-src), [`font-src`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Headers/Content-Security-Policy/font-src)), or, disabling it by setting [`build.assetsInlineLimit: 0`](/config/build-options#build-assetsinlinelimit) is necessary.

old_column.content: Do not allow `data:` for [`script-src`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Headers/Content-Security-Policy/script-src). It will allow injection of arbitrary scripts.

new_column.content: Do not allow `data:` for [`script-src`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Headers/Content-Security-Policy/script-src). It will allow injection of arbitrary scripts.

old_column.content: Vite can generate a file of all the dependencies' licenses used in the build with the [`build.license`](/config/build-options.md#build-license) option. It can be hosted to display and acknowledge the dependencies used by the app.

new_column.content: Vite can generate a file of all the dependencies' licenses used in the build with the [`build.license`](/config/build-options.md#build-license) option. It can be hosted to display and acknowledge the dependencies used by the app.

old_column.content: This will generate a `.vite/license.md` file with an output that may look like this:

new_column.content: This will generate a `.vite/license.md` file with an output that may look like this:

old_column.content: The app bundles dependencies which contain the following licenses:

new_column.content: The app bundles dependencies which contain the following licenses:

old_column.content: To serve the file at a different path, you can pass `{ fileName: 'license.md' }` for example, so that it's served at `https://example.com/license.md`. See the [`build.license`](/config/build-options.md#build-license) docs for more information.

new_column.content: To serve the file at a different path, you can pass `{ fileName: 'license.md' }` for example, so that it's served at `https://example.com/license.md`. See the [`build.license`](/config/build-options.md#build-license) docs for more information.

old_column.content: > Features listed below are automatically applied as part of the build process and there is no need for explicit configuration unless you want to disable them.

new_column.content: > Features listed below are automatically applied as part of the build process and there is no need for explicit configuration unless you want to disable them.

old_column.content: Vite automatically extracts the CSS used by modules in an async chunk and generates a separate file for it. The CSS file is automatically loaded via a `<link>` tag when the associated async chunk is loaded, and the async chunk is guaranteed to only be evaluated after the CSS is loaded to avoid [FOUC](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Flash_of_unstyled_content#:~:text=A%20flash%20of%20unstyled%20content,before%20all%20information%20is%20retrieved.).

new_column.content: Vite automatically extracts the CSS used by modules in an async chunk and generates a separate file for it. The CSS file is automatically loaded via a `<link>` tag when the associated async chunk is loaded, and the async chunk is guaranteed to only be evaluated after the CSS is loaded to avoid [FOUC](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Flash_of_unstyled_content#:~:text=A%20flash%20of%20unstyled%20content,before%20all%20information%20is%20retrieved.).

old_column.content: If you'd rather have all the CSS extracted into a single file, you can disable CSS code splitting by setting [`build.cssCodeSplit`](/config/build-options.md#build-csscodesplit) to `false`.

new_column.content: If you'd rather have all the CSS extracted into a single file, you can disable CSS code splitting by setting [`build.cssCodeSplit`](/config/build-options.md#build-csscodesplit) to `false`.

old_column.content: Vite automatically generates `<link rel="modulepreload">` directives for entry chunks and their direct imports in the built HTML.

new_column.content: Vite automatically generates `<link rel="modulepreload">` directives for entry chunks and their direct imports in the built HTML.

old_column.content: In real world applications, Rollup often generates "common" chunks - code that is shared between two or more other chunks. Combined with dynamic imports, it is quite common to have the following scenario:

new_column.content: In real world applications, Rollup often generates "common" chunks - code that is shared between two or more other chunks. Combined with dynamic imports, it is quite common to have the following scenario:

old_column.content: In the non-optimized scenarios, when async chunk `A` is imported, the browser will have to request and parse `A` before it can figure out that it also needs the common chunk `C`. This results in an extra network roundtrip:

new_column.content: In the non-optimized scenarios, when async chunk `A` is imported, the browser will have to request and parse `A` before it can figure out that it also needs the common chunk `C`. This results in an extra network roundtrip:

old_column.content: Vite automatically rewrites code-split dynamic import calls with a preload step so that when `A` is requested, `C` is fetched **in parallel**:

new_column.content: Vite automatically rewrites code-split dynamic import calls with a preload step so that when `A` is requested, `C` is fetched **in parallel**:

old_column.content: It is possible for `C` to have further imports, which will result in even more roundtrips in the un-optimized scenario. Vite's optimization will trace all the direct imports to completely eliminate the roundtrips regardless of import depth.

new_column.content: It is possible for `C` to have further imports, which will result in even more roundtrips in the un-optimized scenario. Vite's optimization will trace all the direct imports to completely eliminate the roundtrips regardless of import depth.
